before: John Birks 'Dizzy' Gillespie was a trumpet virtuoso who co-founded bebop with Charlie Parker and became one of jazz's greatest ambassadors. His technical mastery allowed him to execute dazzling runs at breakneck tempos, while his harmonic innovations helped define the bebop language. Beyond bebop, Gillespie was instrumental in developing Afro-Cuban jazz, collaborating with Cuban percussionist Chano Pozo to create groundbreaking fusions like 'Manteca' and 'A Night in Tunisia.' Known for his bent trumpet, puffed cheeks, and ebullient personality, he balanced virtuosity with showmanship and humor. Gillespie mentored countless young musicians and led State Department-sponsored tours that spread jazz worldwide, remaining a vital force in music until his death in 1993.
after: John Birks 'Dizzy' Gillespie was a trumpet virtuoso who co-founded bebop with Charlie Parker and became one of jazz's greatest ambassadors. His technical mastery allowed him to execute dazzling runs at breakneck tempos, while his harmonic innovations helped define the bebop language. Beyond bebop, Gillespie was instrumental in developing Afro-Cuban jazz, collaborating with Cuban percussionist Chano Pozo on the fusion 'Manteca'; his composition 'A Night in Tunisia,' written in 1942 while he was with Earl Hines's band, is noted for its syncopated bass line. Known for his bent trumpet, puffed cheeks, and ebullient personality, he balanced virtuosity with showmanship and humor. Gillespie mentored countless young musicians, remaining a vital force in music until his death in 1993.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: 'A Night in Tunisia' written 1942 with Earl Hines band, syncopated bass line; death 1993; mentored/influenced many musicians

before: Eddie Palmieri is a pianist and bandleader who is one of the most important figures in Latin jazz and salsa. His aggressive, dissonant piano style drew as much from McCoy Tyner and Thelonious Monk as from traditional Cuban music, creating a uniquely powerful synthesis that revolutionized Latin music. A ten-time Grammy winner, Palmieri's 'Harlem River Drive' album is a landmark fusion of Latin jazz, soul, and funk.
after: Eddie Palmieri was a pianist and bandleader who was one of the most important figures in Latin jazz and salsa. His aggressive, dissonant piano style drew as much from McCoy Tyner and Thelonious Monk as from traditional Cuban music, creating a uniquely powerful synthesis that revolutionized Latin music. He won nine Grammy Awards over his career, most recently for the 2006 album 'Simpático,' and was the founder of the band Harlem River Drive.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: died Aug 6 2025; 9 Grammy Awards total, most recent for 2006 'Simpático'; founder of Harlem River Drive (1970); Monk and McCoy Tyner influences

before: Flora Purim is a Brazilian vocalist whose extraordinary four-octave range and adventurous spirit helped define the sound of jazz-fusion in the 1970s. Her work with Return to Forever, Airto Moreira, and as a solo artist brought Brazilian vocal traditions into dialogue with free jazz, electronic music, and funk. Her albums 'Open Your Eyes, You Can Fly' and 'Butterfly Dreams' are landmarks of Brazilian-influenced jazz fusion.
after: Flora Purim is a Brazilian vocalist whose rare six-octave range and adventurous spirit helped define the sound of jazz-fusion in the 1970s. Her work with Return to Forever, Airto Moreira, and as a solo artist brought Brazilian vocal traditions into dialogue with free jazz, electronic music, and funk. Her albums 'Open Your Eyes, You Can Fly' and 'Butterfly Dreams' are landmarks of Brazilian-influenced jazz fusion.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: 'Purim has a rare six-octave voice'; Return to Forever, Airto Moreira, Butterfly Dreams (1973)

before: Louis Moholo-Moholo is a South African drummer and percussionist who was a founding member of the Blue Notes, the legendary multi-racial group that fled apartheid in the 1960s. In exile in London, he became a central figure in European free improvisation, playing with everyone from the Brotherhood of Breath to Cecil Taylor. His drumming combines the rhythmic vitality of South African music with the freedom of the European avant-garde.
after: Louis Moholo-Moholo was a South African drummer and percussionist who was a founding member of the Blue Notes, the group he formed with Chris McGregor, Johnny Dyani, and others before emigrating to Europe in 1964 and settling in London. In exile, he became a central figure in European free improvisation, playing with everyone from the Brotherhood of Breath to Cecil Taylor. His drumming combined the rhythmic vitality of South African music with the freedom of the European avant-garde. He died in Cape Town on 13 June 2025.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: died Cape Town 13 June 2025; founded Blue Notes with McGregor, Dyani, Moyake, Feza, Pukwana; emigrated 1964, London 1965; Brotherhood of Breath; played with Cecil Taylor

before: Matana Roberts is an alto saxophonist, vocalist, and composer whose multi-chapter 'Coin Coin' series weaves family history, African-American oral tradition, and experimental jazz into works she calls 'sound quilts.' An inheritor of the AACM tradition, Roberts creates music that is simultaneously avant-garde and deeply rooted in the African-American experience. Each chapter of 'Coin Coin' explores different aspects of ancestry and memory through a unique combination of composition, improvisation, and spoken word.
after: Matana Roberts is an alto saxophonist, clarinetist, and composer whose multi-chapter 'Coin Coin' series weaves family history, African-American oral tradition, and experimental jazz. A member of the AACM tradition, Roberts creates music that is simultaneously avant-garde and deeply rooted in the African-American experience. Each chapter of 'Coin Coin' explores different aspects of ancestry and memory through a combination of composition, improvisation, and spoken word.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: uses they/them pronouns throughout; saxophonist and clarinetist (no vocals); AACM member; multichapter Coin Coin project on history, memory, ancestry

before: Ornette Coleman was an alto saxophonist, violinist, and composer who revolutionized jazz by liberating it from chord changes and conventional song forms. His 1959 debut 'The Shape of Jazz to Come' and 1960's 'Free Jazz'—a collective improvisation that gave the movement its name—were initially controversial but proved prophetic. Coleman's self-taught approach and plastic alto saxophone produced a raw, vocal-like tone that divided audiences: some heard noise, others heard pure emotion. His theoretical concept of 'harmolodics'—treating melody, harmony, and rhythm as equal—influenced everyone from Pat Metheny to punk rockers. Coleman continued exploring through electric bands like Prime Time, classical compositions, and collaborations until his death in 2015, remaining one of music's most fearless innovators.
after: Ornette Coleman was an alto saxophonist, violinist, and composer who revolutionized jazz by liberating it from chord changes and conventional song forms. His 1959 Atlantic album 'The Shape of Jazz to Come' and 1960's 'Free Jazz'—a collective improvisation that gave the movement its name—were initially controversial but proved prophetic. Coleman's self-taught approach and plastic alto saxophone produced a raw, vocal-like tone that divided audiences: some heard noise, others heard pure emotion. His theoretical concept of 'harmolodics'—treating melody, harmony, and rhythm as equal—influenced everyone from Pat Metheny to punk rockers. Coleman continued exploring through electric bands like Prime Time, classical compositions, and collaborations until his death in 2015, remaining one of music's most fearless innovators.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: 'The Shape of Jazz to Come' was Coleman's third studio album (1959, Atlantic), debut was 'Something Else!!!!' (1958); Free Jazz (1960); plastic Grafton alto; Prime Time; harmolodics; died 2015

before: Pat Martino was a guitar virtuoso whose remarkable story includes relearning his instrument from scratch after brain surgery for a near-fatal aneurysm in 1980 erased his memory. Before the surgery, he had established himself as one of the most technically accomplished guitarists in jazz with a fluid, horn-like approach rooted in hard bop. His comeback was one of jazz's great triumphs of human will, and he continued to play at the highest level for decades after his recovery.
after: Pat Martino was a guitar virtuoso whose remarkable story includes relearning his instrument from scratch after a hemorrhaged arteriovenous malformation in 1980, and the surgery that followed, erased his memory. Before the surgery, he had established himself as one of the most technically accomplished guitarists in jazz with a fluid, horn-like approach rooted in hard bop. His comeback was one of jazz's great triumphs of human will, and he continued to play at the highest level for decades after his recovery.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: 1980 hemorrhaged arteriovenous malformation, near-fatal seizure; surgery removed part of brain, left amnesia, had to completely re-learn guitar; guitarist (1944-2021)

before: Roy Ayers is a vibraphonist whose jazz-funk innovations with Ubiquity made him one of the most sampled artists in hip-hop history. Starting as a hard bop vibraphonist influenced by Milt Jackson, Ayers pivoted to funk in the 1970s, creating a sound that merged jazz sophistication with danceable grooves. Songs like 'Everybody Loves the Sunshine' and 'Running Away' became hip-hop building blocks, sampled by artists from A Tribe Called Quest to Mary J. Blige. Ayers' warm vibes tone, smooth vocals, and positive energy created a template for contemporary jazz-influenced R&B. Now in his 80s, he continues performing and has seen his influence acknowledged by generations of musicians who grew up on his sampled legacy.
after: Roy Ayers was a vibraphonist whose jazz-funk innovations with Ubiquity made him one of the most sampled artists in hip-hop history. Starting as a post-bop vibraphonist, Ayers pivoted to funk in the 1970s, creating a sound that merged jazz sophistication with danceable grooves. Songs like 'Everybody Loves the Sunshine' and 'Running Away' became hip-hop building blocks, widely sampled by rappers. Ayers' warm vibes tone, smooth vocals, and positive energy created a template for contemporary jazz-influenced R&B. He continued to perform live until 2023 and died in March 2025 at the age of 84.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: died Manhattan March 4 2025 at 84; performed live until 2023; vibraphonist, began as post-bop; Ubiquity; 'Everybody Loves the Sunshine', 'Running Away'; among most-sampled by rappers

before: Ryo Kawasaki is a Japanese guitarist and electronic music pioneer whose jazz-funk fusion albums became cult classics. His guitar synthesizer explorations anticipated later electronic jazz.
after: Ryo Kawasaki was a Japanese jazz-fusion guitarist and one of the first musicians to develop and popularise the fusion genre, whose albums became cult classics. He helped develop the guitar synthesizer in collaboration with Roland and Korg, and created the Kawasaki Synthesizer for the Commodore 64. He died on 13 April 2020.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: Japanese jazz fusion guitarist (1947-2020), died April 13 2020; among first to develop/popularise fusion; guitar synthesizer with Roland and Korg; Kawasaki Synthesizer for Commodore 64

before: Sonny Rollins is a tenor saxophonist whose massive sound, thematic improvisation, and uncompromising artistic standards have made him jazz's greatest living legend. His 1956 album 'Saxophone Colossus' established him as Coltrane's equal, featuring the calypso 'St. Thomas' which became his signature tune. Famous for his sabbaticals—including a period practicing on the Williamsburg Bridge walkway to avoid disturbing neighbors—Rollins has repeatedly withdrawn from the scene to reassess his music. His improvisations are architectural marvels, building on motifs and developing themes with a logic that connects him to Coleman Hawkins and the saxophone's deepest traditions. Now in his 90s, Rollins remains revered as a musician who never stopped challenging himself.
after: Sonny Rollins was a tenor saxophonist whose massive sound, thematic improvisation, and uncompromising artistic standards made him one of jazz's most revered figures, often called 'the greatest living improviser.' His 1956 album 'Saxophone Colossus' featured the calypso 'St. Thomas,' which became his signature tune. Famous for his sabbaticals—including a period practicing on the Williamsburg Bridge walkway to avoid disturbing neighbors—Rollins repeatedly withdrew from the scene to reassess his music. His improvisations are architectural marvels, building on motifs and developing themes with a logic that connects him to Coleman Hawkins, his idol, and the saxophone's deepest traditions. Rollins died in May 2026, leaving more than sixty albums as a leader.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: died May 25 2026; often called 'the greatest living improviser'; Saxophone Colossus (1956) with 'St. Thomas' calypso; Coleman Hawkins his idol; 60+ albums as leader

before: Sons of Kemet was a British jazz group led by saxophonist and clarinetist Shabaka Hutchings, featuring tuba and two drummers in a lineup that drew on Caribbean carnival traditions and African diasporic music. Their politically charged albums addressed topics from immigration to colonialism, and their explosive live performances made them figureheads of the 2010s London jazz renaissance. The group disbanded in 2023 at the height of their influence.
after: Sons of Kemet was a British jazz group led by saxophonist and clarinetist Shabaka Hutchings, featuring tuba and two drummers in a lineup that drew on Caribbean carnival traditions and African diasporic music. Their albums addressed politically charged themes, and their explosive live performances made them figureheads of the 2010s London jazz renaissance. The group disbanded in 2022 at the height of their influence.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: disbanded 2022 (announced 1 June 2022; span 2011-2022); Shabaka Hutchings sax/clarinet, tuba, two drummers; mix of jazz, rock, Caribbean folk, African music

before: Sun Ra claimed to be from Saturn and led his Arkestra for over 40 years, pioneering Afrofuturism and space jazz. His cosmic philosophy and experimental music challenged every convention. From swing to electronic music to free jazz, Sun Ra explored it all while wearing Egyptian regalia and preaching interplanetary consciousness.
after: Sun Ra claimed to be from Saturn and led his Arkestra from the mid-1950s until his death in 1993, pioneering Afrofuturism and space jazz. His cosmic philosophy and experimental music challenged every convention. From swing to electronic music to free jazz, Sun Ra explored it all while wearing Egyptian regalia and preaching interplanetary consciousness.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: led The Arkestra from the mid-1950s until his death (1914-1993); pioneer of Afrofuturism; eclectic music from ragtime/swing to bebop, free jazz, fusion; Egyptian-inspired costumes

before: Terence Blanchard is a trumpeter and composer who has extended the Jazz Messengers tradition while becoming one of Hollywood's most respected film composers. Taking over Art Blakey's trumpet chair alongside fellow New Orleans native Wynton Marsalis, he developed a warm, singing tone and dramatic sensibility that transferred naturally to cinema. His collaboration with Spike Lee has produced over 20 film scores, while his concert works like 'A Tale of God's Will' documented Hurricane Katrina's devastation. In 2021, his opera 'Fire Shut Up in My Bones' became the first by a Black composer staged at the Metropolitan Opera. Blanchard continues to lead powerful groups, mentor young musicians, and compose music that addresses social justice.
after: Terence Blanchard is a trumpeter and composer who has extended the Jazz Messengers tradition while becoming one of Hollywood's most respected film composers. Succeeding fellow New Orleans native Wynton Marsalis in Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers in 1982, he developed a warm, singing tone and dramatic sensibility that transferred naturally to cinema. His collaboration with Spike Lee has produced the scores for most of Lee's films, while his concert works like 'A Tale of God's Will' documented Hurricane Katrina's devastation. In 2021, his opera 'Fire Shut Up in My Bones' became the first by a Black composer staged at the Metropolitan Opera. Blanchard continues to lead powerful groups, mentor young musicians, and compose music that addresses social justice.
evidenceUsed: wiki extract: in 1982 Marsalis recommended Blanchard as his replacement in Blakey's Jazz Messengers; scored most of Spike Lee's films; A Tale of God's Will (Katrina); Fire Shut Up in My Bones first opera by a Black composer at the Met (2021-22)
